title: C# SOLID — Paper 9
description: Covers DIP concepts, DIP implementation, SRP concepts, SRP refactoring, OCP concepts, Unit Testing & SOLID, SOLID Real C# Scenarios, Dependency Injection Patterns, Strategy Pattern, and Factory & Abstract Factory.

questions.text: ST11-11: In Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, which method allows you to provide a factory delegate for creating a service?

options.text: services.AddFactory<T>()
services.AddTransient<T>(sp => new T(sp.GetRequiredService<IDep>()))
services.AddCreator<T>()
services.RegisterFactory<T>()

questions.text: ST20-15: An ASP.NET Core controller has 15 injected constructor parameters. Which SOLID principle does this most violate?

options.text: OCP — the controller is not open for extension.
SRP — the controller is handling too many concerns; it should delegate to focused services.
ISP — each parameter should be a narrower interface.
DIP — the controller should not use constructor injection.

questions.text: ST13-10: A `ConnectionFactory` class creates either a `SqlConnection` or a `MySqlConnection` based on a config value. Which pattern does this implement?

options.text: Abstract Factory — it creates related families of objects.
Factory Method — a single factory method returns one of several product implementations based on configuration.
Builder — it constructs a connection step by step.
Prototype — it clones an existing connection.

questions.text: ST01-10: Which of the following demonstrates SRP applied to a C# controller in ASP.NET Core?

options.text: A controller that contains all business logic, data access, and validation for an entity.
A thin controller that delegates to injected services for business logic, validation, and data access.
A controller with over 20 action methods for all CRUD operations.
A controller that directly queries a database using SqlConnection.

questions.text: ST19-16: Which NuGet package is the most widely used mocking framework for C# unit tests?

options.text: NUnit.Mocks
Moq
xUnit.Fakes
MSTest.Stubs

questions.text: ST09-14: In a Clean Architecture design, the dependency rule states that:

options.text: Outer layers (infrastructure) can depend on inner layers (domain), but inner layers cannot depend on outer layers.
Inner layers (domain/application) can depend on outer layers (infrastructure).
All layers must depend on the infrastructure layer.
Dependencies must flow from domain to infrastructure and back.

questions.text: ST03-10: When is it acceptable to modify an existing class without violating OCP?

options.text: Never — OCP says classes must never change.
When fixing a bug in the existing behavior (not adding new behavior), or when the class has not yet been published/used by clients.
Whenever the class is private.
When the modification is backward compatible.

questions.text: ST02-10: After identifying that `UserService` has SRP violations, you extract `UserNotifier`. What is the next step?

options.text: Delete UserService and replace it with UserNotifier.
Define an IUserNotifier interface, implement it in UserNotifier, and inject IUserNotifier into UserService via its constructor.
Make UserNotifier a static class.
Have UserService inherit from UserNotifier.

questions.text: ST12-10: When implementing Strategy pattern in C#, where should the strategy selection logic reside?

options.text: Inside each concrete strategy class.
In a factory or the composition root, not inside the context class itself, to keep the context SRP-compliant.
In the base interface.
It does not matter where the selection logic resides.

questions.text: ST20-16: A C# class `ReportManager` depends on `new PdfExporter()`, `new CsvExporter()`, and `new EmailSender()` — all concretions. Which refactoring resolves the DIP violations?

options.text: Add abstract keyword to ReportManager.
Introduce IExporter and IEmailSender interfaces; inject implementations via constructor; register in DI container.
Make all three concretions singletons.
Use the Service Locator pattern inside ReportManager.

questions.text: ST09-15: Which of the following is a correct statement about DIP and interfaces defined in the domain layer?

options.text: Domain interfaces should reference infrastructure types.
Domain interfaces represent abstractions owned by the domain; infrastructure classes implement them, making infrastructure depend on domain, not vice versa.
Domain layer should have no interfaces; only abstract classes.
DIP does not apply to domain-layer design.

questions.text: ST10-10: In Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, `IServiceScope` is used to:

options.text: Register services with a singleton lifetime.
Create a child scope so that scoped services can be resolved outside of an HTTP request context (e.g., in a background service).
Validate the service registrations at startup.
Dispose of transient services manually.

questions.text: ST01-11: Which refactoring tool in Visual Studio/Rider most directly helps identify SRP violations?

options.text: Rename Symbol
Code metrics / dependency analysis — high coupling count and low cohesion highlight multi-responsibility classes.
Generate Unit Tests
Format Document

questions.text: ST11-12: In ASP.NET Core, `IOptions<T>` is used to:

options.text: Register a service with multiple implementations.
Inject strongly-typed configuration sections into classes via the DI system, following DIP by depending on an abstraction over configuration.
Provide optional constructor parameters.
Enable lazy initialization of singleton services.

questions.text: ST19-17: Which of the following is the best definition of 'code coverage' in unit testing?

options.text: The number of lines of code in the test project.
A measure of what percentage of production code is executed by the test suite, indicating how much of the code has been exercised.
Whether the test assertions pass or fail.
The number of test methods in a test class.

questions.text: ST03-11: OCP is described as the 'most important' principle by some authors. Why?

options.text: It makes code compile faster.
It aims to make a system robust against change — new functionality is added without touching (and risking breaking) stable, tested code.
It eliminates all bugs.
It minimizes the number of classes in a system.

questions.text: ST13-11: A `LoggerFactory` in C# creates different logger implementations (ConsoleLogger, FileLogger, CloudLogger) based on configuration. This is an example of:

options.text: Abstract Factory — it creates families of related objects.
Factory Method — the factory encapsulates the creation logic and returns the appropriate ILogger implementation.
Builder — it constructs loggers step by step.
Singleton — only one logger instance is created.

questions.text: ST20-17: A developer notices that every time a new report type is needed, they edit `ReportService`, `ReportController`, `ReportViewModel`, and `ReportRepository`. This is which code smell?

options.text: Divergent Change — ReportService changes for many reasons.
Shotgun Surgery — one change ripples across many classes.
Feature Envy — the classes envy each other's data.
God Class — one class does everything.

questions.text: ST02-11: In Domain-Driven Design, 'domain services' are created when:

options.text: A controller becomes too large.
Business logic doesn't naturally belong to a single entity or value object — it represents a cross-cutting domain concern extracted per SRP.
Infrastructure services need to be hidden from the domain.
You want to avoid using interfaces in the domain.

questions.text: ST12-11: The Strategy pattern is most commonly used to replace which C# antipattern?

options.text: Circular dependency between services.
A large switch statement or if-else chain that selects behavior based on a type or mode.
A singleton service that holds global state.
Deep inheritance hierarchies with many levels.

questions.text: ST09-16: 'Stable' and 'volatile' dependencies in DIP refer to:

options.text: The performance characteristics of each dependency.
Stable dependencies (e.g., core .NET libraries) can be depended on directly; volatile ones (e.g., databases, email services) should be accessed through abstractions to ease substitution.
Whether the dependency uses async/await.
The memory allocation behavior of each dependency.

questions.text: ST10-11: When using Scrutor (a popular .NET library), what does `services.Scan(...)` do?

options.text: Scans assemblies for security vulnerabilities.
Automatically discovers and registers service implementations by convention (e.g., all classes implementing their own interface), reducing manual DI registration boilerplate.
Scans for unused services and removes them.
Validates that all registered services can be resolved.

questions.text: ST01-12: An SRP-compliant class should ideally have:

options.text: Exactly one public method.
All its methods and fields related to a single, well-defined purpose — not necessarily just one method.
No more than five fields.
Only static members.

questions.text: ST19-18: In the context of SOLID and testing, what is 'seam'?

options.text: A CSS styling concept used in test reports.
A place in code where behavior can be altered without editing the code itself — e.g., an interface injection point where a test double can be inserted.
A test that runs at the boundary of two systems.
A merge conflict in a test file.

questions.text: ST11-13: Which of the following is an advantage of constructor injection over property injection for mandatory dependencies?

options.text: Constructor injection allows the dependency to be changed after construction.
Constructor injection guarantees the dependency is provided at object creation time, making the object always valid after construction.
Constructor injection is only available in .NET 6+.
Constructor injection allows injecting more than three dependencies.

questions.text: ST13-12: In C#, the IHttpClientFactory pattern (creating HttpClient instances via a factory) addresses which design concern related to factory patterns?

options.text: It uses the Abstract Factory to create different HTTP client families.
It centralises and manages HttpClient instance creation and lifetime (avoiding socket exhaustion), applying factory pattern principles with DIP.
It implements the Singleton pattern for HttpClient.
It creates HttpClient via reflection for dynamic endpoint support.

questions.text: ST03-12: Which of the following is an example of OCP being violated in configuration code?

options.text: Reading a connection string from appsettings.json.
Hardcoding provider-specific logic inside a class so that supporting a new provider requires editing the class's source code.
Using an IOptions<T> abstraction to read configuration.
Registering multiple implementations of the same interface.

questions.text: ST20-18: A method `CalculateShipping(Order o)` accesses `o.Customer.Address.Country.TaxRate`. This chain violates:

options.text: OCP — the method cannot be extended.
Law of Demeter (and indirectly SRP) — the method is navigating deeply into an object graph, becoming tightly coupled to many classes.
ISP — too many types are accessed.
DIP — the method depends on concrete classes.

questions.text: ST02-12: A `PaymentService` class has methods for credit card, PayPal, and bank transfer processing — each with 50+ lines. What SRP + OCP combined refactoring resolves this?

options.text: Break the methods into smaller private methods within PaymentService.
Extract each payment method into a separate class implementing IPaymentProcessor, inject them via DI, and remove the conditional logic from PaymentService.
Add a PaymentType enum and use a switch expression.
Create three subclasses: CreditCardPaymentService, PayPalPaymentService, BankTransferPaymentService.

questions.text: ST12-12: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the Strategy pattern and unit testing?

options.text: Strategy makes testing harder because there are more classes.
Each strategy class can be unit tested independently with focused tests, and the context class can be tested with a stub strategy.
Strategy eliminates the need for unit tests.
Strategies cannot be mocked.
